principle_title: AI amplifies breadth but humans own depth — design for complementarity

counter_arguments.author: Michelle Vaccaro, Abdullah Almaatouq, Thomas Malone — MIT Sloan / Center for Collective Intelligence
counter_arguments.credibility: Published in Nature Human Behaviour (Dec 2024). Preregistered meta-analysis of 106 experiments (370 effect sizes). Gold-standard methodology for this question.
counter_arguments.summary: Human-AI combinations performed significantly WORSE than the best of humans or AI alone (Hedges' g = -0.23). When AI outperformed humans alone, adding humans to the loop produced losses, not gains. This directly challenges the principle's core claim that designing for complementarity yields superior outcomes — the empirical average says complementarity often destroys value rather than creating it.

counter_arguments.author: AlphaProof team at Google DeepMind — published in Nature (Nov 2025)
counter_arguments.credibility: Nature publication. AlphaProof solved IMO 2024 Problem 6, which only 5 of 609 human competitors solved. Formal proofs are machine-verified, eliminating hallucination concerns.
counter_arguments.summary: AlphaProof achieved deep, cumulative mathematical reasoning — not breadth — by using test-time reinforcement learning to generate and learn from millions of problem variants. It solved the hardest competition problem through sustained multi-step formal proof construction, precisely the kind of 'depth' the principle claims humans uniquely own. This suggests AI can achieve depth through compute-intensive search, not just breadth through parallelism.

counter_arguments.author: François Chollet (Google AI researcher, Keras creator) and ARC Prize Foundation
counter_arguments.credibility: Chollet is the leading researcher on measuring AI abstraction/reasoning. ARC-AGI is the gold-standard benchmark for fluid intelligence. 2025 competition had 1,455 teams and 15,154 entries.
counter_arguments.summary: ARC-AGI-2 results show AI reaching 24% on novel abstract reasoning tasks through 'refinement loops' — iterative, per-task program optimization that resembles cumulative depth-building, not breadth-scanning. The defining theme of 2025 is AI systems that improve through iterative refinement on single problems, challenging the assumption that AI's strength is necessarily breadth-first. However, humans still dramatically outperform at ~85%, so this partially supports the principle too.

counter_arguments.author: Systematic review across 8,852 radiological cases — Frontiers in Radiology (2025)
counter_arguments.credibility: Peer-reviewed systematic review and meta-analysis covering multiple subspecialties of radiology, published 2025.
counter_arguments.summary: In specific diagnostic tasks (e.g., lung nodule segmentation, AUROC 94.4%), AI outperformed six radiologists not through breadth but through deeper pattern recognition in narrow domains. This challenges the boundary of the principle: in perceptual depth tasks with large training data, AI may own depth too. The principle may only hold for open-ended reasoning, not perceptual expertise.

counter_arguments.author: Nature News feature (2025) — surveys multiple AI researchers including Sakana AI's 'AI Scientist' team (Oxford/UBC)
counter_arguments.credibility: Nature news feature surveying the field. References Sakana AI's autonomous research loop and Microsoft's Stargate investment in autonomous scientific discovery.
counter_arguments.summary: Autonomous AI research systems (Sakana AI's 'AI Scientist') demonstrated a complete research loop — hypothesis generation, experimental design, code, execution, paper drafting — without human depth-checking. Autonomous labs moved from proof-of-concept to scaled deployment in 2025. This challenges the principle's assumption that humans must 'dig into the hard spots' — autonomous systems are beginning to do sustained, cumulative investigation without human complementarity.

diversity_gaps: Geographic: all sources are from US/UK institutions — missing perspectives from Chinese AI labs (e.g., DeepSeek) which may have different empirical data on depth vs breadth
Missing: labor economist perspective on whether complementarity design actually creates or destroys jobs vs full automation
Missing: cognitive science perspective on whether 'depth' is a meaningful distinction or a folk category that doesn't carve nature at joints
Domain: counter-evidence is concentrated in math/medicine/science — missing data from creative, legal, or strategic domains where depth may behave differently
resolution_notes: Ingested top 2 counter-arguments (Nature meta-analysis + AlphaProof). 8 insights extracted. Principle PRI-260321-AD44 needs revision to account for: (1) naive complementarity destroys value, (2) AI achieves depth in formal domains.

suggested_sources.title: How to Fix the Co-Founder Fights You're Sick of Having — Lessons from Couples Therapist Esther Perel
suggested_sources.author: Esther Perel — world-renowned psychotherapist, bestselling author of 'Mating in Captivity' and 'The State of Affairs', host of 'How's Work?' podcast, organizational consultant to startups and Fortune 500s
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: Perel applies couples therapy frameworks to co-founder dynamics — a genuinely contrarian lens. She argues all co-founder fights reduce to 3 root issues (power/control, care/closeness, respect/recognition) and provides specific therapeutic techniques for navigating them. Published Feb 2024 on First Round Review, one of the highest-quality startup publications. Long-form deep dive with actionable frameworks.

suggested_sources.title: Solo Founders Report 2025 — Carta Data Analysis
suggested_sources.author: Peter Walker, Head of Insights at Carta — analysis based on tens of thousands of U.S. companies incorporated on Carta's platform
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: Deeply contrarian and data-driven. Challenges the YC orthodoxy that you need a co-founder by showing solo founders rose from 24% to 36% of startups (2019-2025), hold 50% larger stakes at Series B, and have 75% greater median ownership at exit. AI is accelerating the trend. This is the strongest empirical challenge to conventional co-founder wisdom available, and directly tension-worthy against existing insights about finding builders and team dynamics.

suggested_sources.title: Psychologist Matt Jones on Why Startups Fail Due to Co-Founder Conflict and How to Fix It
suggested_sources.author: Dr. Matthew Jones, Psy.D. — licensed clinical psychologist specializing exclusively in co-founder dynamics, author of 'The Cofounder Effect', has worked with founders backed by YC, Sequoia, a16z. Interviewed by Mark MacLeod, former CFO of several startups and executive coach.
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: The only psychologist working full-time on co-founder relationships brings clinical rigor to a domain dominated by anecdotal advice. His contrarian claim: two-thirds of startup failure is rooted in co-founder conflict, not product-market fit. He identifies that top-performing teams don't avoid conflict — they navigate it differently. Specific psychological frameworks for diagnosis and repair. Published Nov 2025.

suggested_sources.title: How To Navigate Co-Founder Disputes — YC Lightcone Podcast
suggested_sources.author: Garry Tan (YC President/CEO), Harj Taggar, Jared Friedman, Diana Hu — all YC partners who have collectively seen thousands of co-founder relationships succeed and fail
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: YC partners sharing battle-tested patterns from seeing thousands of startups. Combines multiple perspectives from people who have both been co-founders themselves and advised hundreds of founding teams. Long-form podcast discussion with specific advice on managing co-founder conflict. The YC dataset (7000+ companies) gives these observations statistical weight unavailable elsewhere.

suggested_sources.title: 8 Cofounder Conflict Myths That Sabotage Startups
suggested_sources.author: Dr. Matthew Jones, Psy.D. — Psychology Today 'Scaling Connection' column, clinical psychologist and co-founder coach
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: Explicitly contrarian — debunks 8 widely-held beliefs about co-founder conflict that Jones argues actively sabotage startups. Part of a multi-article Psychology Today series (2025) that also covers hidden dynamics, power struggles, and why speed kills conflict resolution. The myth-busting format is ideal for generating high-stance insights that challenge conventional wisdom. Published April 2025.

resolution_notes: Ingested Esther Perel co-founder article (4 insights). Carta solo founders report blocked (HTTP 403). Remaining 3 sources available for future ingestion.

suggested_sources.title: Why Most AI Products Fail: Key Findings from MIT's 2025 AI Report
suggested_sources.author: Mind the Product, synthesizing MIT FutureTech's 2025 enterprise AI study
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: MIT found 95% of generative AI pilots yield no business impact. The core concept of a 'learning gap' — where tools fail because they don't adapt to enterprise workflows, not because models are bad — is a deeply contrarian reframe. Challenges the assumption that better models = better products. Also reveals that back-office automation succeeds far more than high-visibility AI features, which contradicts most startup playbooks.

suggested_sources.title: OpenAI Product Leader: The 7-Step Playbook for Defensible AI Products
suggested_sources.author: Miqdad Jaffer (OpenAI Product Lead), curated by Kevin Kwok (Creator Economy)
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: From inside OpenAI, the person building the products shares a concrete framework: high-frequency pain selection, data exhaust flywheels, adjacency potential for expansion. The contrarian insight is that speed without defensibility makes you the first target — directly relevant to existing insights about AI wrappers and feature-vs-product distinctions. Practitioner-level depth from the most important AI company.

suggested_sources.title: The 2026 AI Product Strategy Guide: How to Plan, Budget, and Build Without Buying Into the Hype
suggested_sources.author: Mind the Product editorial team
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: Contrarian framing: the risk is no longer missing the AI boat, it's boarding a boat that burns cash with every user interaction. Introduces the 3x value-to-compute-cost rule, AI P&L line discipline, and circuit breaker patterns for agentic systems. Fills gaps around unit economics and cost governance that the existing 5 insights don't cover at all.

suggested_sources.title: The New AI Growth Playbook for 2026: How Lovable Hit $200M ARR in One Year
suggested_sources.author: Elena Verna (growth advisor, ex-Miro/Amplitude/Dropbox) on Lenny's Podcast
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: Elena Verna argues the traditional growth playbook is completely rewritten for AI companies — Lovable focuses on innovation over optimization and gives away their product for free as their most powerful growth strategy. This is deeply contrarian: most product strategy assumes monetization gates, not free-as-growth-engine. Long-form podcast (~60+ min) with concrete examples from a $200M ARR case study.

suggested_sources.title: In Consumer AI, Momentum Is the Moat
suggested_sources.author: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) AI Apps team
suggested_sources.quality_rationale: a16z challenges their own earlier 'Empty Promise of Data Moats' thesis by arguing that in the current AI cycle, momentum itself — speed of iteration, user growth velocity, and category creation pace — is the real moat, not data or technology. Combined with their framework of three AI app categories (AI-native existing categories, labor-replacing new categories, proprietary-data closed-loop workflows), this provides strategic depth the existing insights lack.

resolution_notes: Ingested MIT AI products report (3 insights). Remaining 4 sources available for future ingestion.
